Facts.text: St Miltiades begins his reign as Catholic Pope

Incident at Xuanwu Gate: Prince Li Shimin ambushes and kills his rivals, the brothers Li Yuanji and Li Jiancheng, sons of Emperor Gaozu; he then forces the emperor to name him as successor to the throne
Remains of Chinese Emperor Gaozong, his wife Empress Wu Zetian and family members interred in Qianling Mausoleum by Emperor Zhongzong, outside Chang'an on Mount Liangshan
Yax Pac [First Dawn] 16th and last ruler of the Maya K'inich Yax K'uk Mo' dynasty, ascends to the throne of Copán after the death of his father Smoke Shell - rules till 820
The imperial army proclaims Nicephorus Phocas to be Emperor of the Romans on the plains outside Cappadocian Caesarea
Hartbert becomes bishop of Utrecht
Treaty of Perth: Norway recognises Scottish sovereignty over the Hebrides and the Isle of Man
Battle of Gollheim between warring German kings Albert I of Habsburg and Adolf of Nassau results in Adolf's defeat and death
Turkish naval commander Dragut [Turgut Reis] sacks the Italian city of Paola
Menas, Emperor of Ethiopia, defeats a revolt in Emfraz
Ottoman Empire invades Venetian controlled Cyprus and lays siege to its cities. Gains control of the island August 1571.
Muitende Spanish soldiers conquer Zierik Sea
English explorer Martin Frobisher sights Baffin Island
Battle at Nieuwpoort: Maurice Count of Nassau leads an Anglo-Dutch army to victory over the Spanish led by Albert, Duke of Burgundy
The first English expedition from Massachusetts against Acadia led by Samuel Argall
Battle of Marston Moor, North Yorkshire: Parliamentary forces under Lords Fairfax and Manchester defeat royalists led by Prince Rupert
Europeans first visit Minnesota and see headwaters of Mississippi in an expedition led by Daniel Greysolon de Du Luth
Earl of Shaftesbury arrested for high treason
English engineer Thomas Savery patents the first steam engine
Continental Congress resolves "these United Colonies are, and of right ought to be Free and Independent States"
New Jersey gives the right to vote to all adults who could show a net worth of 50 pounds
Second Battle of Seneffe: France-Austria
Marine Police established by The West India Committee to guard merchant vessels on the river Thames, London, beginnings of the world's oldest continuously serving police force
Simon Fraser completes his trip down Fraser River, British Columbia, lands at Musqueam
Denmark Vesey (aka Telemaque), accused of planning a slave rebellion in South Carolina, dies by hanging at approximately 55
Bahia Independence Day: End of Portuguese rule in Brazil, with the final defeat of the Portuguese crown loyalists in the province of Bahia
Slaves aboard a Spanish schooner La Amistad revolt to secure their freedom while being transported from one Cuban port to another
Envelope bearing 1st US 10 cent stamps used (still exists today)
Benjamin Lane patents gas mask with a breathing apparatus
Partial emancipation of Russian serfs
Battle of Hoke's Run, West Virginia - small Union victory
Lincoln signs act granting land for state agricultural colleges
R Morgan's: Burksville, Kentucky to Salineville, Ohio [->JUL 26]
General Early and Confederate forces reach Winchester en route to Washington, D.C.
Statuary Hall in US Capitol forms
1st US elevated railroad begins service, NYC
Jules Joseph d'Anethan is elected the tenth Prime Minister of Belgium
Canada's North-west Insurrection ends with surrender of Big Bear
US Congress passes Sherman Antitrust Act
Government obtains injunction against striking Pullman Workers
National League MLB batting champion Ed Delahanty, disappears after being removed intoxicated from a train by force; found dead at bottom of Niagara Falls 2 weeks later
Pitcher Jack Doscher, debuts for the Chicago Cubs, the first son of a major leaguer to play MLB, father Herm 1882-92
Yanks win by forfeit for the 1st time
Horn & Hardart opens its 1st NYC "automat" (self-service restaurant)
Erich Muenter, an instructor in German at Cornell University, explodes a bomb in the US Senate reception room
Russian offensive in Armenia
Race riot in East St Louis, Illinois escalates - Black neighborhood attacked and burned, 40-200 killed over 2 days and 6000 left homeless
Ralph Samuelson (18) rides the world's first water skis on Lake Pepin in Lake City, Minnesota, after previous attempts riding a board
US Army Air Corps created; Distinguished Flying Cross authorized, to award "heroism or extraordinary achievement while participating in an aerial flight"
Jericho Earthquake in Palestine kills about 500
British parliament reduces the age at women can vote to 21 - the same as men (Representation of the People Act 1928)
The Jenkins Television Corporation (owned by Charles Jenkins) goes on air with W3XK, the first television broadcasting station in the USA
MLB Chicago White Sox outfielder Carl Reynolds becomes only the 2nd player in MLB history to hit home runs in 3 consecutive innings in 15-4 victory at Yankee Stadium
General Lazaro Cardenas elected president of Mexico
Great Britain amateur boxing team beats United States in the first International Golden Gloves tournament in NYC, New York
24-hour honor guard is first posted at the Tomb of the Unknowns at Arlington National Cemetery in Virginia
Former Dutch PM Hendrikus Colijn publishes "Borders of 2 Worlds" (German victory)
Indian independence leader Subhas Chandra Bose is arrested and detained in Calcutta
Lake Washington (Seattle) Floating bridge dedicated
Nazi mass murder in Lvov/Lemberg (7,000 dead)
Gulf of Biskaje: Liberator bombers sinks U-126
Lieutenant Charles Hall becomes 1st African American pilot to shoot down a Nazi plane
Dutch Beel government forms
Harbor workers end strike at Rotterdam and Amsterdam
Military coup discovered in France
"Red Barber's Clubhouse" sports show premieres on CBS (later NBC) TV
Henri Queuille is elected the seventh Prime Minister of the Fourth French Republic
Kinkaku-ji, a Zen Buddihst temple in Kyoto, Japan, burns down
"Bob & Ray show" premieres on NBC radio
Island advisor of Curacao installed
Leiden astronomers discover a radio signal from outside the Milky Way system
Worcestershire cricket wicket-keeper Hugo Yarnold makes a record 6 stumpings in an innings in a 1st-class match vs Scotland at Dundee
Netherlands' Princess Beatrice opens miniature city of Madurodam
England cricket batsman Denis Compton scores career best 278 in 287 mins in 2nd Test win over Pakistan at Nottingham
"Lawrence Welk Show" premieres on ABC
US performs nuclear test at Enewetak Atoll (atmospheric tests)
First submarine designed to fire guided missiles is launched, the USS Grayback (SSG-574)
Musical drama Film "King Creole" starringElvis Presleybased on a novel by Harold Robbins premieres
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Bikini Island
First French nuclear test on Mururoa Atoll
West Indian cricket batsman Basil Butcher scores career best 209 not out to guide tourists to a 139-run 3rd Test win over England at Trent Bridge
An El Al Israeli airliner is hijacked and diverted to Algeria by three armed members of the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine (PFLP)
American guitarist Leslie West and producer, bassist Felix Pappalardi form rock group Mountain
Ireland cricketers dismiss West Indies for just 25 (Dougie Goodwin 5 for 6, Alec O'Riordan 4 for 18) to set up a huge upset in Londonderry
First Boeing 747 lands in Amsterdam and Brussels
Neil Blaney is found not guilty of illegal arms importation to the Irish Republican Army by a Dublin jury
The Prevention of Incitement to Hatred Act (Northern Ireland) is introduced; it proves difficult to secure convictions under its provisions, seldom enforced
USSR performs underground nuclear test
American pole vaulter Bob Seagren breaks world record for the 4th and final time with 5.63m in Eugene, Oregon
India and Pakistan sign Simla Agreement peace accord
Two Catholic civilians are shot and killed in Belfast by Loyalist paramilitaries, probably the Ulster Defence Association (UDA)
James R. Schlesinger ends his term as the 9th Director of the CIA
Nation Black Network begins operation on radio
Fernando Mameda of Portugal sets record for 10,000 m (27:13.81)
Formal reunification of North and South Vietnam
Ramble Inn attack: the Ulster Volunteer Force killed 6 civilians (5 Protestants, 1 Catholic) in a gun attack at a pub near Antrim; the pub was targeted because it was owned by Catholics
US Supreme Court rules death penalty not inherently cruel or unusual
Comedy film "Airplane!" written and directed by David Zucker, Jim Abrahams, and Jerry Zucker and starring Robert Hays and Julie Hagerty premieres
Grateful Dead guitarist Bob Weir, drummer Mickey Hart, and manager Danny Rifkin arrested for "suspicion of inciting a riot" after a concert in San Diego, California
Julie Marie Bryan, 18, of Georgia, crowned America's Young Woman of Yr
In South Africa, the Internal Security Act is passed, giving massive powers to the authorities to investigate any organization or publication
Larry Walters rises to 16,000 feet over Los Angeles using a lawn chair and 42 helium balloons
Soyuz T-6 returns to Earth
Andrei Gromyko appointed Chairman of the Presidium of the Supreme Soviet in the USSR
European Space Agency launches Giotto (Halley's Comet Flyby)
US Supreme Court upholds affirmative action in 2 rulings
KC Royals outfielder Jim Eisenreich hits 1st HR since 1982, in MLB comeback process, retired 1984 (Tourette syndrome)
Nilde Iotti is named the first female President of the Italian Chamber of Deputies
Lester Dumakude, commander of an Umkhonto we Sizwe special operations unit, detonate a car bomb by remote control outside Ellis Park Stadium in Johannesburg, South Africa
American jockey Steve Cauthen wins the Irish Derby on Old Vic to become the first rider in history to sweep the world's four major Derbies (Kentucky, Epsom, French, Irish)
1,426 pilgrims are trampled to death after a panic in a tunnel in Mecca, Saudi Arabia
TV cooking competition show "MasterChef" presented by Loyd Grossman, and created by Franc Roddam, first airs in the UK
Braniff Airlines goes out of business
Boat sinks at Bocaue Philippines, 325 die
F-28 crashes at Sorong Irian Barat, 41 die
Muslim fundamentalists in Sivas, Turkey, set hotel on fire, kill 36
Royals Stadium in Kansas City, Missouri is renamed Kauffman Stadium shortly before the death of MLB Kansas City Royals team owner Ewing Kauffman
John Wayne Bobbitt & Kristina Elliot arrested for domestic battery
Middlesex cricket seam bowler Richard Johnson takes all 10 Derbyshire wickets for 45 runs at the County Ground in Derby
US Air DC-9 crash in North Carolina, 37 killed
Thailand: Banharn Silpa-Archa's party wins election
"Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets" the second book in the series is published by Bloomsbury in the UK
Vicente Fox is elected the first President of México from an opposition party, the Partido Acción Nacional after more than 70 years of continuous rule by the Partido Revolucionario Institucional
AbioCor self contained artificial heart created.
Bush Administration announce that it will seek to let oil companies drill on about 1,500,000 acres of the Gulf of Mexico
UN Security Council, facing an almost certain Russian veto, agrees to postpone indefinitely a vote on the US-led "smart sanctions" package for Iraq
Steve Fossett becomes the first person to fly solo nonstop around the world in a balloon
ASEAN Regional Forum accepts Pakistan as its 24th member.
Live 8 concert In Mercy Fitzgerald Square, Johannesburg, South Africa; performers include Lucky Dube, Mahotella Queens, Orchestra Baobob, Zola, and Vusi Mahlasela
Live 8 concert in Red Square, Moscow, Russia; performers include Pet Shop Boys, Agata Kristi, Splean, and Red Elvises

A settlement is reached allowing former NBA franchise the Seattle SuperSonics to move to Oklahoma City, Oklahoma; new owner Clay Bennett agrees to pay City of Seattle $45 million to wriggle out of last 2 years of KeyArena lease
Ingrid Betancourt and 14 other FARC hostages are rescued by the Colombian armed forces.
FIFA World Cup: Ghana, only African team remaining in last 8, are beaten 4-2 on penalties by Uruguay; Netherlands upset Brazil 2-1
Oil tanker truck explosion in South Kivu, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, kills at least 230 people
GlaxoSmithKline settles the largest healthcare fraud case in history for US$3 Billion
Monsoon rain in East India kills at least 79 people and leaves 2.2 million homeless
Reds pitcher Homer Bailey no-hits SF Giants 3-0 in Cincinnati, his second no-hitter in 10 months
62 people are killed after the Kim Nirvana ferry capsizes off the coast of the Philippines
BP agrees to compensate US government & gulf states $18.7 billion for the 2010 Gulf of Mexico oil spill
Large lorry bomb in Baghdad kills at least 125 people and wounds 150, Islamic State claims responsibility
FIFA Confederations Cup: Germany defeats Chile 1-0 to win for the 1st time at Krestovsky Stadium in Saint Petersburg, Russia
Siege of Roche-au-Moine ends, part ofKing Johnof England's attempt to reclaim Normandy from France
After an encounter with a violent thunderstorm,Martin Lutherdeclares that he will become a monk
Battle of Yamazaki:Toyotomi HideyoshidefeatsAkechi Mitsuhide
KingJames IIdisbands English parliament
At the Continental Congress,John Dickinsonabstains from the votes that declare independence
Marquis de Sadeshouts from Bastille that prisoners are being slaughtered

French frigate "La Méduse" runs aground under incompetent leadership of Viscount of Chaumareys, 400 passengers evacuated. 150 men, 1 woman are left on "la Machine," a poorly provisioned raft. 13 days later only 15 survivors remain, inspiresThéodore Géricault's painting "The Raft of the Medusa"
Giuseppe Garibaldibegins hunger strike in Rome
US PresidentJames Garfieldshot by Charles Guiteau a disappointed office-seeker; Garfield dies 79 days later
First flight of Zeppelin LZ-1, a dirigible airship designed by GrafFerdinand von Zeppelin, at Lake Constance near Friedrichshafen, Germany
Jean Sibelius' musical tone poem "Finlandia" premieres in Helsinki
Butch Cassidyand the Sundance Kid rob a train of $40,000 in Wagner, Montana
Detroit Tigers legendTy Cobbhits in his 40th straight game in 14-6 rout of Cleveland. Streak ends next game
Leninsays Imperialism is caused by capitalism

In boxing's first million dollar gate ($1.7m), world heavyweight championJack DempseyKOs Frenchman Georges Carpentier in round 4 of his 3rd title defense; crowd 91,000 at Boyle's Thirty Acres, Jersey City
Warren G. Hardingsigns a joint congressional resolution declaring the official end of war with Germany
American golferWalter Hagenwins his 5th and last Western Open in Cleveland, 16 years after he won his first
FDRdelivers the first acceptance speech at a presidential nominating convention
NY Giants pitcherCarl Hubbellworks 18 innings of shutout ball without a walk to beat St Louis Cardinals, 1-0
Amelia Earhartand Fred Noonan disappear flying over the Pacific Ocean en route to Howland Island. Noonan is declared dead the following year, and Earhart is declared the year after (1939)
BritishPM Churchillmeets Major GeneralBernard Montgomery
Joe DiMaggiohits a 3-run homer off Boston's Dick Newsome to pass Willie Keeler's MLB record 44 game hitting streak
Noël Coward's "Blithe Spirit" premieres in London
German Field MarshalGünther von Klugereplaces General von Rundstedt
The State of Vietnam is internationally recognised, governing the southern half of Vietnam, withBảo Đạias chief of state
Cleveland Indians' pitcherBob Fellerwins his 200th MLB game, 5-3 over Detroit Tigers
Bill Veeckbuys MLB St Louis Browns from Bill & Charlie DeWitt; after 1953 season sells franchise to Clarence Miles, who move team Baltimore (Orioles)
Elvis Presleyrecords "Hound Dog" and "Don't Be Cruel" at RCA studio in New York City
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Le pelerinage De Lourdes
Amidst 5 NY Yankee homers v Washington,Roger Marishits 29th and 30th en route to 61 for the season
Sam Waltonopens his first Walmart store in Rogers, Arkansas
SF Giants legendWillie Maysbreaks up classicJuan MarichalvsWarren Spahnpitching dual, homering in 16th inning off Spahn for 1-0 win vs Milwaukee Braves
ArchitectLe Corbusieris made Grand Officiers of the Légion d'honneur
Cilla Black records Lennon/McCartney song "It's For You",Paul McCartneyplays piano
Grand jury indictsByron De La Beckwithin murder of Medger Evers
US PresidentLyndon B. Johnsonsigns the Civil Rights Act and the Voting Rights Act into law
American jazz musicianLouis Armstrongleads his All-Star band in a performance at the BBC television studio in London, England; the thirteen song set is broadcast in September
"You've Never Been This Far Before" single released byConway Twitty(Billboard Song of the Year - Country, 1973)
MLB pitcherRon Guidrybeats Detroit 3-2 for a NY Yankee record 13-0 start to the season
Susan B. Anthonydollar is issued, first US coin to honor a woman
Boston Red Sox pitcherRoger Clemens(14-0) suffers his 1st loss (4-2 v Toronto), one win short of AL record for consecutive wins to start season
General strike againstPinochetregime in Chile
Tiananmen Square student leaderWang Danarrested and imprisoned for spreading counterrevolutionary propaganda and incitement (trial not till 1991)
Imelda Marcosand Adnan Khashoggi found not guilty of racketeering
Donald Trumpproposes toMarla Maplesand gives her a 7.5 carat diamond ring
Film "Boyz n the Hood" directed by John Singleton, starringCuba Gooding Jr.andIce Cubeis released
PopeJohn Paul IIhospitalized for Cat Scan test
Ringo Starr's third All-Starr Band debuts in concert; members include: Randy Bachman, Mark Farner,Billy Preston, Felix Cavaliere, John Entwistle, Zak Starkey, and Mark Rivera
Silvio Berlusconi, Prime Minister of Italy, insults German MP Martin Schulz by calling him a "kapo" during a session of the European Parliament.
10 Live 8 concerts held around the world organised byBob Geldofto raise awareness of poverty
Michael Schumacherwins US F1 Grand Prix for the 5th time, 1st driver in any series to win 5 races at Indianapolis Motor Speedway
Sri Lanka'sSanath Jayasuriya(152) and Upul Tharanga (109) compile world record cricket ODI opening stand of 286 vs England at Headingley
16 people are killed and 200 are injured in protest clashes at Cairo University against Egyptian PresidentMohamed Morsi
Alex Puccioascends Top Notch in Rocky Mountain National Park, her first V13 (8B) graded boulder
Former French PresidentNicolas Sarkozyis criminally charged with corruption by French prosecutors
Home town underdog Jeff Horn upsetsManny Pacquiaoof the Philippines on points in a highly controversial WBO welterweight title fight in Brisbane, Australia
An alligator falls from the sky during a thunderstorm in Charleston, South Carolina
2nd day of the Battle of Gettysburg (US Civil War)
One-time Methodist Reform Church ministerWilliam Boothand his wife Catherine Booth found the East London Christian Mission, now known as The Salvation Army
Valentinian III, Roman emperor (425-55), born in Ravenna (d. 455)
Al-Mustanṣir, 8th Fāṭimid Caliph of Cairo, born in Cairo, Egypt (d. 1094)
Maria, Queen of Sicily and Duchess of Athens & Neopatria, born in Catania, Kingdom of Sicily (d. 1401)
Thomas Cranmer, England reformer, Archbishop of Canterbury (1533-55), martyr, born in Aslockton, England (d. 1556)
Elizabeth Tudor, English princess, second daughter and fourth child of Henry VII of England and Elizabeth of York, born in Richmond Palace, Surrey, England (d. 1495)
Johann Staden, German baroque organist and composer, baptized in Free Imperial City of Nuremberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1634)
Guillaume Messaus, Flemish composer, born in Antwerp, Dutch Republic (d. 1640)
Daniel Speer, German composer, born in Breslau, Habsburg Empire (d. 1707)
Abraham a Santa Clara [Johann Ulrich Megerle], German Augustinian friar and preacher, born in Kreenheinstetten, Germany (d. 1709)
Daniel Finch, 2nd Earl of Nottingham, English Tory statesman, born in London (d. 1730)
Guillielmus "Willem" Kerricx, Flemish sculptor (Rozenkrans), born in Dendermonde, East Flanders (d. 1719)
Samuel Penhallow, English-born American colonist and historian, born in St Mabon, Cornwall, England (d. 1726)
Pietro Ottoboni, Italian cardinal and grandnephew of Pope Alexander VIII, born in Venice (d. 1740)
Francesco III d'Este, Duke of Modena & Reggio, born in Modena, Duchy of Modena and Reggio (now Italy) (d. 1780)
Christoph Willibald Gluck, Austrian composer (Il re pastore), born in Weidenwang, Electoral Palatinate, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1787)
Friedrich Gottlieb Klopstock, German poet (Der Messias), born in Quedlinburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1803)
François Léonard Rouwyzer, Dutch composer, born in Maastricht, Dutch Republic (d. 1827)
Hardenack Otto Conrad Zinck, Danish flautist, composer, choral director, and music educator (Blaagaard Seminarium, 1791-1811), born in Husum, Duchy of Schleswig, Danish Crown (d. 1832)
Christian Gottfried Körner, German lawyer, born in Leipzig (d. 1831)
Peter Ritter, German cellist and composer, born in Mannheim, Electorate of the Palatinate, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1846)
Antoine Prumier, French harpist and composer, born in Paris (d. 1868)
Robert Toombs, American lawyer and 1st Confederate States Secretary of State, born in Washington, Georgia (d. 1885)
Thérèse Wartel, French pianist and composer, born in Paris (d. 1865)
Charles-Louis Hanon, French composer and piano pedagogue (The Virtuoso Pianist in 60 Exercises), born in Renescure, France (d. 1900)
Thomas Anderson, Scottish chemist (discovered pyridine), born in Leith, Scotland (d. 1874)
George Law Curry, American newspaper publisher and Governor of Oregon, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1878)
Douglas Strutt Galton, English engineer (rails/trains), born in Hadzor House, Worcestershire, England (d. 1899)
John Bordenave Villepigue, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Camden, South Carolina (d. 1862)
Hendrick Peter Godfried Quack, Dutch lawyer, economist (Bank of the Netherlands) and historian, born in Zetten, Netherlands (d. 1917)
Henry Eugene Davies, American writer, public official, lawyer, and Major General (Union Army), born in New York City (d. 1894)
Marcel Bertrand, French geologist and mine engineer (tectonic geology), born in Paris (d. 1907)
Maria Theresa of Austria-Este, Queen of Bavaria, born in Brno, Austrian Empire (d. 1919)
Francesco Spetrino, Italian conductor and composer, born in Palermo, Italy (d. 1948)
William Henry Bragg, English physicist  (Nobel Prize for Physics, 1915 - crystalline structure analysis by means of X-rays), born in Westward, Cumberland, England (d. 1942)[1]
Lily Braun [Amalie von Kretschmann], German feminist writer (Im Schatten Titanen), and socialist politician, born in Halberstadt, Saxony, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1916)
Herbert Prior, British actor (Caught Short, Slave of Desire), born in Oxfordshire, England (d. 1954)
Wilhelm Cuno, German Reich's chancellor (1922-23) and businessman, born in Suhl, German Empire (d. 1933)
Albert Szirmai, Hungarian operetta composer, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1967)
Royal H. Weller, American politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from New York), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 1929)
Edgar Mayne, Australian cricketer (Australian batsman, prolific for Victoria), born in Jamestown, South Australia (d. 1961)
Alfons Maria Jakob, German neurologist, born in Aschaffenburg, Bavaria (d. 1931)
Marcel Tabuteau, French-American oboist (Philadelphia Orch 1915-54), born in Compiègne, Oise, France (d. 1966)

Selman Waksman, Ukrainian-American microbiologist (Nobel 1951-ingenious, systematic and successful studies of the soil microbes that led to the discovery of streptomycin), born in Nova Pryluka, Kiev Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1973)[1]
Cor Hermus, Dutch actor, director, and writer (Bleeke Bet; William of Orange; De Jantjes; A Mother), born in Amsterdam, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1953)
Earl Roy Curry, American religious thinker and Kirtland Temple overseer, born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1980)
Gus Dorais, American College Football Hall of Fame quarterback (Notre Dame), coach (Gonzaga, Detroit Lions), born in Chippewa Falls, Wisconsin (d. 1954)
Jack Hylton, English orchestra leader and impresario (Crazy Passage Show), born in Great Lever, Lancashire, England (d. 1965)
Ralph Hancock, Welsh garden designer (Rockefeller Center), born in Cardiff, Wales (d. 1950)
Lydia Mei, Estonian artist who specialized in watercolors and still lifes, born in Hiiumaa, Estonia (d. 1965)
Anthony McAuliffe, American general and commander of the 101st Airborne Division celebrated for his reply of "Nuts!" to a German surrender ultimatum, born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1975)
Tyrone Guthrie, British actor, producer, writer and director, born in Tunbridge Wells, England (d. 1971)
Rex Alston, English sports broadcaster and author (BBC radio), born in Faringdon, Berkshire (d. 1994)
Olav V, King of Norway (1957-91), born in Appleton House, Sandringham, Norfolk (d. 1991)
Carl Weinrich, American organist, choral conductor, Baroque revivalist, and educator, born in Paterson, New Jersey (d. 1981)
Gerarda Rueter, Dutch sculptor, born in Amsterdam (d. 1993)
Alan Webb, British stage and screen character actor (King Lear; Taming of Shrew; Challenge of Lassie), born in York, England (d. 1982)
Hans Bethe, German-American quantum physicist (Nobel 1967), born in Strassburg, Germany (d. 2005)
Robert Levine Sanders, American composer, and organist, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1974)
Eppo Doeve, Dutch cartoonist and painter, born in Bandung, Indonesia (d. 1981)
Leo O'Brien, Australian cricket batsman (5 Tests; Victoria), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1997)
Jean Sinclair, South African campaigner, founder member and first president of the Black Sash in 1955, born in Germiston, South Africa (d. 1996)
Hermann Bengtson, German historian (Greeks Ancient Times), born in Ratzeburg, Germany (d. 1989)
Earl Robinson, American composer (Ballad for Americans; Preamble to Peace), and folk music singer-songwriter ("Joe Hill"'; "Black and White"), born in Seattle, Washington (d. 1991)
H. G. Adler, Czech-English writer (The Journey; Theresienstadt 1941-1945), Holocaust survivor. and poet, born in Prague, Bohemia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1988)
William Douglas Denny, American composer, born in Seattle, Washington (d. 1980)
Diego Fabbri, Italian playwright and leader (Vatican movie bureau), born in Forli, Italy (d. 1980)
Edith Heerdegen, German actress (Serpent's Egg, Monpti), born in Dresden, Germany (d. 1982)
Frederick Fennell, American conductor (Time & Winds), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2004)
Mário Schenberg, Brazilian physicist and electrical engineer, born in Recife, Pernambuco, Brazil (d. 1990)
Bert Decorte, Flemish poet, born in Retie, Flanders, Belgium (d. 2009)
Barry Gray [Bernard Yaroslaw], American radio personality and interviewer (started call-in radio), born in Red Lion, New Jersey (d. 1996)

Hans-Ulrich Rudel, German Luftwaffe fighter pilot, the highest-decorated German soldier of WWII, founder of South American Nazi relief organization, and military consultant, born in Konradswaldau, German Empire (d. 1982)
Ken Curtis, American actor (Ripcord, Festus - Gunsmoke), born in Lamar, Colorado (d. 1991)
Pierre H. Dubois, Dutch literary and critic, born in Amsterdam (d. 1999)
"Sheikh" Imam [Eissa], Egyptian folk and protest singer-songwriter, born in Giza (d. 1995)
WiBo [Willem Boost], Dutch cartoonist, born in Breda, Netherlands (d. 2005)
Jean Craighead George, American writer (Julie of the Wolves), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 2012)
Eliseo Diego, Cuban poet, born in Havana, Cuba (d. 1994)
Maria Britneva, Russian actress (A Room with a View, Maurice), born in Petrograd, Russia (d. 1994)
Genrikh Matusovich Vagner, Belarusian composer, born in Żyrardów, Poland (d. 2000)
Pierre Cardin, Italian-French fashion designer, born in San Biagio di Callalta, Italy (d. 2020)
Wislawa Szymborska, Polish poet referred to as the 'Mozart of Poetry' (Nobel Prize for Literature 1996), born in Bnin, Poland (d. 2012)
Rick Besoyan, American singer, actor, playwright, composer and director (Little Mary Sunshine), born in Reedley, California (d. 1970)
Marvin Rainwater, American country singer (Ozark Jubilee), born in Wichita, Kansas (d. 2013)
Billy Usselton, American big band jazz saxophonist (Les Brown and His Band of Renown), born in New Castle, Pennsylvania (d. 1994)
Lee Allen, American tenor sax player (Walkin' With Mr Lee), born in Pittsburg, Kansas (d. 1994)
Octavian Paler, Romanian writer and journalist who criticized the Communist regime, born in Lisa, Romania (d. 2007)
Brock Peters [George Fisher], American actor and singer (Carmen Jones, To Kill a Mockingbird), born in New York City (d. 2005)
Charlie White American professional baseball catcher (Philadelphia Stars; Milwaukee Braves), born in Kinston, North Carolina (d. 1998)
Ruth Berghaus, German choreographer and opera director, born in Dresden, Germany (d. 1996)
Estelita Rodriguez, Cuban actress (Rio Bravo, Cuban Fireball, Havana Rose), born in Guanajay, Cuba (d. 1966)
Line Renaud [Jacqueline Ente], French pop and cabaret singer, actress, and AIDS activist, born in Pont-de-Nieppe, France
Ahmad Jamal [Frederick Jones], American jazz pianist, composer, bandleader and educator, born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2023)[1]
Carlos Menem, Argentine politician, President of Argentina (1989-99), born in Anillaco, Argentina (d. 2021)
Frank Williams, English actor (Dad's Army - "Reverend Farthing"), born in Edgware, Middlesex, England (d. 2022)
Robert Ito, Canadian-born actor (Quincy, ME - "Sam"), born in Vancouver, British Columbia
Dave Thomas, American businessman who founded Wendy's Restaurants, born in Atlantic City, New Jersey (d. 2002)
Kenneth McMillan, American actor (Malone, Concrete Beat), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1989)
David Lewin, American composer, born in New York City (d. 2003)
Kalim Siddiqui, Pakistani-British writer and Islamic activist, born in Dondi Lohara, British India (d. 1996)
Ivan Madray, West Indian cricket leg-spin all rounder, 2 Tests 3 runs 0-108), born in Port Mourant, Guyana (d. 2009)
